Reform Research Project
William Lloyd Garrison

1805 — 1879

Journalist. Abolitionist. Editor of The Liberator for 35 years. He demanded the immediate end of slavery and refused to be silent — or to compromise.

Explore Research View Timeline
William Lloyd Garrison

Reform Research Project · Joshua Curtis · US History I Honors · Period 7 · Shrewsbury High School · 2026

01 · Biography 02 · Context 03 · Obstacles 04 · Action Plan 05 · Take Action 06 · Results 07 · Today 08 · Reflection

Section 01

Background & Biography

William Lloyd Garrison was born on December 10, 1805 in Newburyport, Massachusetts. He was an abolitionist and journalist who spent basically his entire adult life fighting to end slavery. He published The Liberator, an anti-slavery newspaper, every single week for 35 years.

His dad was a sailor who left when Garrison was only 3. A trade dispute with Britain shut down the port and he lost his job — and then he just kind of dissapeared. So Garrison grew up pretty poor. He and his brother literally sold candy and clams on the street to help out. By age 13 he was already working at a newspaper, teaching himself everything as he went.

In 1829 he moved to Boston and started co-editing an abolitionist paper with a guy named Benjamin Lundy. He got thrown in jail for calling out a local merchant who was involved in the slave trade — which honestly just made him more determined. After getting out he started The Liberator on January 1, 1831, and that was basically his whole life from then on.

"I will be as harsh as truth, and as uncompromising as justice."

— William Lloyd Garrison · The Liberator, Vol. 1, No. 1 · 1831

William Lloyd Garrison · c. 1870s

Section 02

Historical Context

Garrison grew up during a time when slavery was just... normal to most people, especially in the South. The whole southern economy was built around it. Growing up in New England with pretty strong Christian values, Garrison genuinly couldn't understand how people were okay with it.

When he started out, basically nobody wanted to hear what he had to say. Southerners hated abolitionists. But honestly a lot of Northerners did too — they thought people like Garrison were just making things worse. Even in free states, most people had kind of accepted that slavery was just the way things were.

The Liberator — Garrison's newspaper, published 1831–1865

Gradualism vs. Immediate Emancipation

Most abolitionists at the time, including Lundy who Garrison worked with, thought the best approach was slow and gradual. Garrison completely disagreed. In the very first issue of The Liberator he basically said — I'm not waiting, I'm not softening my language, and I will not stop. Slavery is wrong and it needs to end now.

The Political Climate

Garrison thought the whole political system was basically rigged to protect slavery — which honestly wasn't wrong. So he refused to vote, refused to join any political party, and even called the Constitution a "covenant with death." His whole thing was that laws don't change until people's beliefs change first. So he focused entirely on changing minds.

Section 03

Obstacles Faced

External Opposition

Violence and Mobs — In 1835, an angry mob in Boston literally dragged Garrison through the streets and almost killed him. He'd already been jailed earlier for calling out a merchant involved in the slave trade. None of it made him back down — if anything it made him louder.

Nobody Really Liked Him — Southerners portrayed abolitionists as dangerous radicals. But even a lot of Northerners thought Garrison was just stirring things up for no reason. He was kind of an outcast from all directions for most of his career.

Public Opinion Was Against Him — Saying slavery needs to end immediately was considered pretty extreme at the time. For a long time, most Americans thought he was just a radical troublemaker. He spent decades being ignored or hated before people started taking him seriously.

Internal Challenges

Splits Inside the Movement — As Garrison started connecting abolition to women's rights and other causes, a lot of people in the movement got uncomfortable and left. The split with Frederick Douglass was probably the most famous — they had worked together closely and then completely fell out over strategy.

Health and Money — In his later years, poor health and money problems slowed him down a lot. He wasn't able to be as active as he'd been earlier in his career.

Garrison attacked by an anti-abolitionist mob in Boston · 1835

Section 04

Action Plan

Specific Goal

Garrison's goal was simple — end slavery, and end it now. He had zero interest in gradual emancipation or waiting around. Everything he did was pointed at that one thing.

Intended Audience

Northern Americans — people in free states who were mostly just ignoring slavery or telling themselves gradual change was good enough.

The general public — he wanted to get his message in front of as many people as possible, especialy in the North where he thought minds could actually be changed.

Other reform movements — he connected abolition to women's rights and other causes, which brought more people in but also caused a lot of conflict.

Strategies Planned

His main plan was pretty straightforward — write as much as possible, speak publicly as much as possible, and build organizations to amplify everything. He believed if enough people heard the argument clearly, they'd have to agree.

An abolitionist addressing a crowd · 19th century engraving

Section 05

Taking Action

What He Actually Did

For over 35 years Garrison just kept going. The Liberator came out every week without fail. He gave speeches all over the place, even in cities where people were actively hostile to him. He had alredy been traveling and speaking before the paper even existed — the newspaper was kind of the next level of something he was already doing.

As the Civil War got closer he started actually engaging with politics — condemning the Kansas-Nebraska Act, denouncing the Dred Scott decision, and eventually supporting Lincoln. When the Emancipation Proclamation came out he welcomed it even though it wasn't the full immediate abolition he'd wanted his whole life. And when the 13th Amendment passed in 1865 he published the final issue of The Liberator and wrote: "My vocation as an abolitionist is ended." Just like that, 35 years later, he was done.

Strategies He Used

The Liberator (1831–1865) — Every week for 35 years. Direct, uncompromising, and he never took money from advertisers so nobody could tell him what to write.

American Anti-Slavery Society (1833) — He helped organize the AASS to coordinate the movement across the country — petitions, lectures, pamphlets. It grew into one of the most important reform organizations of the era.

Public Speaking — He gave speeches everywhere, even in cities where people showed up specifically to cause problems. He genuinly did not care.

Moral Suasion — His whole philosophy was that laws follow beliefs, not the other way around. Change what people think and the laws will catch up. He stuck to this for decades, then finally shifted toward political action when the Civil War made it unavoidable.

Key Alliances

Benjamin Lundy — Garrison's first major mentor. They co-edited together before splitting over gradual vs. immediate emancipation.

Frederick Douglass — Garrison helped bring Douglass into the spotlight early on. They later split when Douglass concluded that political action was necessary and Garrison still held firm to moral suasion only.

Isaac Knapp — Co-publisher of The Liberator from the very first issue. Knapp handled the business side that kept the paper running.

Legislation

For most of his career Garrison wouldn't touch politics at all. He thought the whole system was corrupt and that moral persuasion was the only real path. Eventually the Civil War changed that and he started engaging — supporting Lincoln, welcoming the Emancipation Proclamation, and ultimately seeing the 13th Amendment passed in 1865, which was literally the thing he'd been working toward his entire life.

Section 06

Results & Impact

35
Years Publishing
1865
Slavery Abolished
13th
Amendment

The Liberator was genuinly one of the most influential anti-slavery publications in American history. Garrison kept slavery in the national conversation for decades — at a time when most people in power would have been perfectly happy to just ignore it and move on.

After the Civil War his influence faded a bit — he was in poor health and the movement had changed. But the goal he started fighting for in 1831 was accomplished. Slavery was abolished. The 13th Amendment passed. He literally lived to see it happen.

Northern Anti-Slavery SentimentMajor
Years of The Liberator35 yrs
Impact on National DebateFoundational
Abolition Goal AchievedComplete

William Lloyd Garrison, later in life

Section 07

Connection to Today

What Garrison figured out in the 1830s is basically still how activism works today. You use media, you make a moral arguement, you apply public pressure, and you keep going even when people tell you you're being too extreme. That idea never realy went away.

BLM is probably the most obvious connection. Like Garrison, they used media and moral pressure to force the country to actually look at racial injustice instead of ignoring it. Garrison had The Liberator; BLM had social media. Both got called too radical, too angry, too disruptive — the exact same critisisms Garrison heard his whole career. Turns out that's just what people say when they don't want to deal with the problem.

Theres a statue of Garrison on Commonwealth Avenue in Boston now — like a few miles from where a mob literally tried to kill him in 1835. That's kind of the whole story right there. The people society calls dangerous radicals in one generation are usually the ones it puts statues of in the next.

Garrison statue — Commonwealth Avenue, Boston

He spent 35 years being called dangerous, irresponsible, and extreme. He got jailed. A mob chased him through the streets of Boston. And now there's a bronze statue of him sitting in a chair on one of the nicest streets in the city. He always said moral truth would win out eventually — he was right, it just took a while.

His playbook — media, coalitions, and never compromising on the core principle — is still how most serious activist movements operate today.

Section 08

Reflection

Essential Question

"How effective was William Lloyd Garrison in bringing about change in nineteenth century society?"

William Lloyd Garrison was highly effective in bringing about change in 19th century society because he kept slavery in the national conversation for over 35 years, helped build the abolitionist movement from almost nothing, and lived to see the 13th Amendment abolish slavery in 1865 — which was the exact thing he had been fighting for since he was 25 years old.

Was He Successful?

Yes, but not right away. For most of his career Garrison was seen as an extremist and people on both sides thought he was going too far. But the things he pushed for — immediate emancipation, equal rights, women being allowed to speak at public events — eventualy became things most people agreed with. The 13th Amendment abolished slavery in 1865, which was literaly the goal he started fighting for in 1831. Those are real, concrete results.

What Lasting Impact Did He Have?

Garrison is remembered as one of the most important abolitionists in American history. His statue stands on Commonwealth Avenue in Boston today. Beyond slavery, he also supported women's rights and suffrage, connecting the two biggest reform movements of his era. His basic idea — that you should use media and public pressure to change what people believe, not just pass laws — is still how most activist movements work today.

What Would I Have Done Differently?

If I were Garrison, I probaly would of worked inside the political system a little earlier. He refused to vote or support any political party for most of his life because he thought the whole system was corupted by slavery. I get why, but I think it also slowed things down. If he had supported political abolitionists earlier, maybe slavery could of ended before the Civil War even started. Then again, maybe his whole power came from not playing by the rules — so its hard to say if working within the system would of made him more effective or just made him easier to ignore.

William Lloyd Garrison

Fun Facts

9 Things You Might Not Know

Jailed Before The Liberator Existed

In 1830, before he even founded his newspaper, Garrison was thrown in jail for publicly calling out a Massachusetts merchant involved in the slave trade. He sat in jail for 49 days — and came out more determined than ever.

Why Did His Father Leave?

Garrison's dad left when William was only 3. The U.S. Embargo Act of 1807 was meant to hurt Britain but destroyed American shipping jobs. His father Abijah was a merchant sailor who lost his livelihood almost overnight. Without work, he eventualy just dissapeared. Its probaly one of the reasons Garrison fought so hard for people the system left behind.

He Grew Up Selling Candy

After his father left, young Garrison and his brother sold molasses candy and clams to help his mother get by. At 10 he became a cobbler's apprentice, and by 13 he was already working at a newspaper. He taught himself most of what he knew.

He Called the Constitution Evil

Garrison publicly called the U.S. Constitution a "covenant with death and an agreement with hell" because it protected slavery. He once burned a copy of it at a rally. This shocked even many abolitionists at the time.

He Refused to Vote for 30+ Years

Garrison believed the entire American political system was so corrupted by slavery that working within it was pointless. He refused to vote or support political parties for most of his adult life. He only changed his mind when the Civil War made political action unavoidable.

He Walked Out of a Convention

At the 1840 World Anti-Slavery Convention in London, Garrison refused to participate after women delegates were barred from the floor. He sat in the gallery with the excluded women in protest — a bold move that connected the abolitionist and women's rights movements.

His Newspaper Never Missed a Week

The Liberator published every single week from January 1, 1831 to December 29, 1865 — 35 straight years. Garrison wrote most of it himself and never accepted money from advertisers to protect its independence.

He and Douglass Fell Out

Garrison was one of Frederick Douglass's earliest supporters and helped launch his career. But they eventually had a major falling out — Douglass concluded that political action was necessary, while Garrison held firm to moral persuasion alone. They never fully reconciled.

"My Vocation Is Ended"

When slavery was abolished in 1865, Garrison published the final issue of The Liberator and wrote simply: "My vocation as an abolitionist is ended." He had spent 35 years working toward that exact moment. He was 60 years old.

William Lloyd Garrison

Reform Research Project · Joshua Curtis · US History I Honors · Period 7 · Shrewsbury High School · 2026

Timeline

Key Events in Garrison's Life

1805
Born in Newburyport, Massachusetts
Born December 10, 1805 at 3 School Street, Newburyport, Massachusetts. His father abandoned the family when he was 3. He and his brother sold candy and clams to survive.
William Lloyd Garrison's house — Roxbury, Boston
1818
Becomes a printer's apprentice
At age 13, Garrison became an apprentice at the Newburyport Herald, learning the trade that would define his life. He taught himself to write by setting type — reading every word he composed.
1829
Co-edits Genius of Universal Emancipation
Moved to Boston and co-edited the abolitionist paper with Benjamin Lundy. This is where he developed his views on immediate emancipation — and eventually split with Lundy over it.
1830
Jailed for 49 days
Garrison was thrown in jail for publicly attacking a Newburyport merchant involved in the slave trade. He served 49 days and came out more determined than before. This was the moment he decided he would not moderate his language.
1831
Founds The Liberator
On January 1, 1831, Garrison published the first issue of The Liberator with co-founder Isaac Knapp. The opening line declared he would be "as harsh as truth, and as uncompromising as justice." He kept that promise for 35 years.
1833
Founds the American Anti-Slavery Society
Garrison helped organize the AASS in Philadelphia — one of the most important abolitionist organizations in American history. It coordinated petitions, lectures, and pamphlets across the country.
1835
Attacked by a mob in Boston
An anti-abolitionist mob in Boston attacked Garrison and nearly lynched him. He was dragged through the streets before being locked up for his own protection. He was back at his desk the next day.
1863
Supports Lincoln and the Emancipation Proclamation
After years of refusing to engage with politics, Garrison endorsed Lincoln and welcomed the Emancipation Proclamation. He saw it as an important step — even if it wasn't the full immediate abolition he had demanded.
1865
Slavery abolished — final issue of The Liberator
The 13th Amendment abolished slavery in 1865. Garrison published the final issue of The Liberator the same year, writing: "My vocation as an abolitionist is ended." He had spent 35 years working toward exactly this moment. He resigned from the AASS and considered his work complete.
1879
Death in New York City
William Lloyd Garrison died on May 24, 1879 in New York City at the age of 73. He is buried at Forest Hills Cemetery in Boston. His statue stands today on Commonwealth Avenue — one of Boston's most prominent public spaces.
William Lloyd Garrison

Gallery

Photos & Images

Oil Portrait
c. 1850s

A color oil portrait of Garrison from around the 1850s, near the height of his influence. He was in his forties and had been publishing The Liberator for over two decades by this point.

Formal Portrait
c. 1870s

A formal black and white photograph taken late in Garrison's life, likely in the 1870s. By this point slavery had been abolished and he had retired from The Liberator, but his legacy was already being recognized.

The Boston Mob Attack
1835

An engraving depicting the 1835 anti-abolitionist mob attack in Boston. Garrison was dragged through the streets and nearly killed. The attack actually drew more attention to his cause than it suppressed it.

The Liberator Masthead
1831–1865

The famous masthead of The Liberator, Garrison's abolitionist newspaper. It ran every week for 35 years without missing a single issue. The masthead featured imagery of slavery and freedom that made its message immediately clear.

Public Address
19th Century

An engraving of an abolitionist speaker addressing a crowd. Garrison was an accomplished public speaker and gave hundreds of lectures throughout his career, even in cities where crowds were hostile.

Garrison Statue
Commonwealth Avenue, Boston

The bronze statue of Garrison on Commonwealth Avenue — a few miles from where he was attacked by a mob in 1835. He is depicted seated, holding a book, looking out over the street. The inscription on the base reads: "I am in earnest — I will not equivocate — I will not excuse — I will not retreat a single inch — and I will be heard."

William Lloyd Garrison

About

About This Project

JC
Joshua Curtis
US History I Honors · Period 7 · 2026

Hi, I'm Joshua, a sophomore at Shrewsbury High School. This website is my Unit 5 Reform Research Project for Mr. Braz's US History I Honors class, Period 7.

I chose Garrison because I thought his story was actually pretty interesting. He spent basically his whole life being told he was too extreme and too radical, and then he turned out to be right. That's a good story.

Outside of school I'm mostly into dogs and golf. This project was a lot of work but I genuinly learned alot — especially about how change actually happens and why it takes so long.
